reworded.name: User-centered direction
reworded.old: The user is the subject of the copy, not the product or the situation. Copy puts the user in motion toward a concrete outcome. CTAs name the literal thing the user gets after clicking — not the action, not a metaphor, not wordplay. Benefits are framed as what the user gains, not what they stop worrying about.
reworded.new: The user is the subject of the copy, not the product or the situation. Copy puts the user in motion toward a concrete outcome. CTAs name the literal result the user gets after clicking — not the action they're performing, not a metaphor, not clever wordplay. The user should know what happens before they click. Benefits are framed as what the user gains, not what they stop worrying about.

reworded.name: Functional completeness
reworded.old: Every standalone piece of copy does its full job without depending on another element to close the loop. Messages name the problem, provide relevant context, and point to the next action — all within the same unit. A user who sees only that one element should have enough to act.
reworded.new: Every standalone piece of copy does its full job without depending on another element to close the loop. Error messages follow the sequence: WHAT HAPPENED, WHY, WHAT TO DO — in that order. A vague error like 'Something went wrong' is a product failure, not just a copy failure. Confirmation dialogs name the specific consequence, not a generic prompt. Empty states guide the user toward their first action rather than announcing absence.

reworded.name: Tonal calibration
reworded.old: Copy hits a precise register: calm and grounded without being clinical, warm without being breezy, confident without cheerleading. The target is the way a competent colleague would talk to you after a snag — plain, direct, present. The copy should contain at least one beat that sounds like a person actually said it, and the rhythm should vary so payoffs land like an exhale rather than another list item.
reworded.new: Tone shifts deliberately based on context. Celebratory moments (first successful payment, onboarding complete) can be warm. Error states should be calm and precise — never panicked, never dismissive. Routine actions should be nearly invisible. Applying a single flat tone across all contexts feels robotic and erodes trust. No marketing register inside the product itself.

dimensions_changed.name: User-centered direction
added_dims: Instructions use active voice — 'Enter your account number' not 'Your account number should be entered'
Button labels describe the outcome, not the action — 'Save changes' over 'Submit', 'Start free trial' over 'Sign up'
removed_dims: Buttons name the concrete, literal result of clicking — not a vague verb, metaphor, or clever phrase

dimensions_changed.name: Functional completeness
added_dims: Empty states direct the user toward their first action — they read as a helpful nudge, not a dead end
Confirmation dialogs name the specific consequence and scope — 'Delete 3 invoices permanently?' not 'Are you sure?'
Error messages follow the what-why-what structure: what happened, why it happened, what to do next — no generic fallback copy

dimensions_changed.name: Tonal calibration
added_dims: Product UI copy does not use promotional or marketing register — no superlatives, no value-prop language inside the flow
Celebratory moments (first payment, milestone reached) are allowed to be warm — tone matches the emotional weight of the moment
Error and warning states read as steady and precise — not panicked, not clinical, not sarcastic
Avoids pep-talk language, forced optimism, or performative warmth in neutral or error contexts
Register varies intentionally across screens — not one flat temperature throughout
removed_dims: Register varies intentionally — not one flat temperature throughout; payoff moments land like an exhale, not a list item
Contains at least one moment that sounds like something a real person would say out loud — offhand and plain, not performing conversational
Reads as steady and present — not clinical, not breezy, not sardonic, not like a support article
Avoids pep-talk language, forced optimism, or performative warmth

dimensions_changed.name: Trust through specificity
added_dims: Terminology is consistent across the entire flow — the same action, object, or state is never referred to by two different names

reasoning: I made three main sets of changes. First, I expanded 'Functional completeness' to explicitly capture my error message structure (what-why-what), specific confirmation dialogs, and actionable empty states — these were completely missing and they're non-negotiable for me. Second, I rewrote 'Tonal calibration' to reflect that tone should shift by context rather than stay flat — the old version described one target register, but I actually want warmth in celebratory moments and precision in errors, not the same 'competent colleague' tone everywhere. Third, I added a consistency dimension to 'Trust through specificity' and a no-passive-voice dimension to 'User-centered direction' since inconsistent terminology and passive instructions are dealbreakers I kept running into that the rubric wasn't catching.

reviewed_changes.reason: User-centered direction — cta_names_outcome (dimension updated: 'Button labels describe the outcome, not the action'): 'Add a Payee' names the action the user performs; 'Save Your First Payee' names what they have afterward — a saved payee — and the word 'First' also reinforces this is an empty-state nudge toward a first action, satisfying the new Functional completeness empty-state dimension
reviewed_changes.user_feedback: I see what it's going for, but 'Save Your First Payee' feels a little hand-holdy to me. The 'Your First' part is doing too much — it's trying to be encouraging but it ends up sounding like it's narrating the user's experience back at them. Also, this label would be wrong the second they've already saved a payee and come back to add another. 'Add Payee' is actually fine here — it's an outcome ('a payee gets added') and it's clean. I'd maybe push it to 'Add New Payee' if we need to distinguish it, but I wouldn't ship 'Save Your First Payee'.

added.name: Terminology consistency
added.description: The same thing is always called the same thing, everywhere in the flow. If the UI calls it a 'payment', no screen in the same flow calls it a 'transaction' or a 'transfer'. Inconsistent terminology creates silent doubt — the user wonders if these are different things. No placeholder text ships to production.

reworded.name: User-centered direction
reworded.old: The user is the subject of the copy, not the product or the situation. Copy puts the user in motion toward a concrete outcome. CTAs make it clear what clicking achieves — simple, literal labels are preferred when the action IS the outcome. Benefits are framed as what the user gains, not what they stop worrying about.
reworded.new: The user is the subject of the copy, not the product or the situation. Copy puts the user in motion toward a concrete outcome. CTAs describe what clicking achieves, not the mechanical action being performed — the user should know what happens before they click. Benefits are framed as what the user gains, not what they stop worrying about.

reworded.name: Tonal calibration
reworded.old: Tone shifts deliberately based on context. Celebratory or first-time moments can carry warmth. Error states should be calm and precise — never panicked, never dismissive. Routine actions should be nearly invisible. No marketing register inside the product. The target is how a competent, slightly knowing colleague would talk to you — plain, direct, present. Never a pep talk, never a shrug.
reworded.new: Tone shifts deliberately based on context. Celebratory or first-time moments can carry warmth. Error states should be calm and precise — never panicked, never dismissive. Routine actions should be nearly invisible. No marketing register inside the product. Applying the same tone everywhere feels robotic — the copy should match the emotional weight of the moment.

dimensions_changed.name: Functional completeness
added_dims: Error messages follow the what-why-what structure: what happened, why it happened, what to do next — no generic fallback copy like 'Something went wrong'
removed_dims: Error messages follow the what-why-what structure: what happened, why it happened, what to do next — no generic fallback copy

dimensions_changed.name: User-centered direction
added_dims: Button labels describe the outcome, not the action — 'Save changes' over 'Submit', 'Start free trial' over 'Sign up'; no metaphors or clever wordplay
removed_dims: Button labels make it clear what clicking achieves — 'Add Payee' is fine when a payee being added IS the outcome; 'Resubmit order' over 'Try again'; no metaphors or clever wordplay

dimensions_changed.name: Tonal calibration
added_dims: Routine, repetitive actions use copy that is as invisible as possible — no warmth or personality injected into mundane confirmations

dimensions_changed.name: Contextual presence
removed_dims: Contains at least one moment that sounds like something a real person would say out loud — offhand and plain, not performing conversational

reasoning: I made three main changes. First, I added a dedicated 'Terminology consistency' criterion at priority 4 — inconsistent terminology and placeholder text shipping to production are dealbreakers for me, and they were only loosely implied before, not explicitly called out. Second, I bumped 'Contextual presence' down to priority 6 and removed the 'one_human_beat' dimension, which felt too prescriptive and a bit at odds with my preference for invisible routine copy. Third, I added a dimension under 'Tonal calibration' to explicitly call out that routine actions should be tonally invisible, since the description mentioned it but no dimension enforced it.

reviewed_changes.reason: User-centered direction (cta_names_outcome dimension updated to 'describe the outcome, not the action'): 'Start Sending Payments' labels the mechanical action the user is initiating; 'Set Up Payments' names what the user will have when the interaction is complete — a payment schedule configured and ready. This aligns with the updated standard ('Save changes' over 'Submit') without overpromising or using marketing register.
reviewed_changes.user_feedback: I actually prefer 'Start Sending Payments' here. 'Set Up Payments' sounds like you're configuring a settings page, not initiating a real transfer. The user's goal in this moment is to send money — that's the outcome they care about. 'Set Up' undersells it and feels like there's more work ahead before anything actually happens. I know the rubric says name what the user will have when it's done, but the outcome here IS sending payments, not having a payment schedule configured. The original was closer to the truth.
